title: The Role of Somatic Experiencing in Psychedelic-Assisted Therapy
body:
The Role of Somatic Experiencing in Psychedelic-Assisted Therapy

It all started with a tightness in my chest. Not the kind you get from running a race, but something heavier, like an old wound quietly pulsing beneath the surface. I was midway through my first guided psilocybin journey, the room dim and quiet. As the mushrooms began to unfurl their peculiar dance within my mind, memories long buried started creeping up - snapshots of anxiety, trauma, and unresolved tension. But amidst the swirling thoughts, it wasn’t just my head that was reacting. My body spoke in its own language: a subtle tremor in my hands, a heat rising in my belly, a sudden urge to breathe deeper. That was the moment I realized the story wasn’t just in my brain. It was in my body too.

That embodied experience was my first real encounter with the concept of somatic experiencing during psychedelic therapy. It’s easy to think the magic of psychedelics happens solely in the mind - visions, thoughts, insights. But as I’ve come to learn over years of working with these medicines, the body carries an equal, sometimes louder narrative. It never forgets, and it often holds keys to healing that words can’t reach. The tension in my chest wasn’t just anxiety; it was the residue of past trauma, stored silently until unlocked by the psychedelic’s gentle nudge.

Psychedelic-assisted therapy can feel like a voyage into the self, but somatic experiencing means paying attention to the vessel you’re traveling in. When you engage the body’s sensations, movements, and rhythms, you begin to understand trauma and healing on a level the intellect alone can’t grasp. It’s something I wish more people knew before setting foot on the psychedelic path. Because the body remembers what the mind tries to forget, and any deep healing must honor that truth.

It might sound strange to some - how can shaking, trembling, or tension be part of therapy? But to me, this is where the deepest work lies. The body doesn’t lie. During my sessions, I learned to listen not just to the whispers of my psyche but to the murmurs of muscle, breath, and heartbeat. This shift in awareness opened a doorway to healing that words and thoughts alone never unlocked. If you’re curious about how the physical body weaves into psychedelic therapy, this article will take you through that journey - the science, the research, and the practical ways to bring somatic wisdom into your own experience.

What Is Somatic Experiencing and How Does It Connect to Psychedelic Therapy?

Somatic experiencing, developed by Peter Levine, is a body-oriented therapeutic approach that focuses on the physiological effects of trauma and stress. Rather than diving straight into the story or memories, it invites you to feel the body’s sensations - the tightness, the heat, the tingling. Trauma doesn’t just live in the mind as a narrative. It’s embedded in the nervous system and expressed through bodily symptoms. Somatic experiencing helps release this trapped energy by guiding awareness to the body’s subtle cues, facilitating a process of discharge and integration.

When you combine this with psychedelic-assisted therapy - a treatment model increasingly validated by science - you open a channel for deep healing. Psychedelics like psilocybin or MDMA are known to reduce activity in the brain’s default mode network, which quiets the inner critic and loosens rigid mental patterns. This shift creates a fertile ground for accessing deep emotions and memories under the safety of a broadened state of consciousness. But without attuning to the body, many of these experiences risk remaining abstract or unresolved.

The nervous system is incredibly complex, divided into branches like the sympathetic (fight or flight), parasympathetic (rest and digest), and the newer polyvagal theory’s social engagement system. Trauma often jams the nervous system into states of hyperarousal or shutdown. Psychedelics, through their neurochemical effects, can bring these states into awareness. Somatic experiencing provides tools to track and regulate these bodily responses, helping the nervous system renegotiate its balance rather than getting stuck in old survival patterns.

Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind touches on the importance of the physical experience during psychedelic sessions but doesn’t explore somatic approaches in depth. What somatic experiencing adds is a map for understanding how the body holds trauma, and a method for gently releasing it through guided attention to sensation and movement. When paired, these approaches can enhance emotional regulation, deepen insights, and support lasting transformation.

By engaging the body, somatic experiencing shifts therapy from a purely cognitive process to a lived, felt experience. Since trauma often manifests as physical symptoms - chronic pain, tension, digestive issues - addressing the body directly can reduce reliance on talk therapy alone. The psychedelic journey becomes more than a mental excursion; it becomes a complete mind-body encounter.

Research and Evidence Supporting Somatic Experiencing in Psychedelic Therapy

While the intersection of somatic experiencing and psychedelic therapy is still emerging, the individual fields have substantial evidence supporting their efficacy. Peter Levine’s work on somatic experiencing stems from decades of clinical practice showing trauma’s imprint on the body and how releasing somatic energy can restore balance and reduce PTSD symptoms. His method focuses on titration - slowly moving through sensations to prevent overwhelm and promote nervous system regulation.

On the psychedelic front, the last decade has seen rapid growth in studies demonstrating the therapeutic potential of psilocybin, MDMA, and other compounds for PTSD, depression, and anxiety. For instance, the work from MAPS (Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies) on MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD highlights how the drug’s effects on fear circuitry and social bonding facilitate trauma processing. While these studies primarily focus on psychological outcomes, many therapists incorporate somatic techniques during sessions to stabilize and deepen the work.

A recent pilot study by Kuhn et al. (2022) explored the integration of body-centered therapies with psychedelic treatment. They found that somatic practices helped participants remain grounded during intense emotional releases, enhancing overall safety and efficacy. This aligns with earlier trauma research, including van der Kolk’s seminal book The Body Keeps the Score, where he documents how trauma rewires the brain and nervous system, making somatic interventions essential.

Another study by Mithoefer et al. (2019) on MDMA-assisted psychotherapy noted that therapists who incorporated breathwork, movement, and other somatic techniques reported greater patient stabilization during sessions. They emphasized how somatic listening helps manage the physical intensity of psychedelic states, preventing retraumatization while allowing access to deep emotional material.

Neuroscientifically, Carhart-Harris’s research at Imperial College London reveals how psychedelics decrease rigid brain network activity, creating space for neural plasticity. Somatic experiencing could complement this by guiding the nervous system through physical sensations that correspond with these mental shifts, promoting rewiring on both mind and body levels. The two methods combined suggest a promising pathway to not just understanding trauma intellectually, but feeling and releasing it completely.

Applying Somatic Experiencing Techniques in Your Psychedelic Practice

If you’re preparing for a psychedelic session or looking to deepen your current practice, incorporating somatic experiencing techniques offers a practical way to stay present and grounded. The first step is cultivating body awareness - something that might feel unfamiliar if you’re used to thinking primarily in words or images during trips. Before your session, try simple body scans, where you bring gentle attention to various body parts, noticing sensations without judgment.

During the journey, pay attention to shifts in your breathing, pulse, temperature, or muscle tension. For example, if you feel tightness in your shoulders or a flutter in your stomach, breathe into that space rather than trying to push it away. Some people find it helpful to gently move - rocking back and forth, shaking limbs, or stretching - to help the energy flow. This doesn’t mean forcing movement but responding to natural urges your body might have to discharge tension.

Journaling can be a powerful partner to somatic work. After sessions, writing about what you physically experienced helps process and integrate sensations alongside thoughts. I recommend keeping a dedicated notebook like this Journaling Notebook Leather Bound to track your body-centered reflections. Over time, you’ll notice patterns and learn which somatic cues relate to specific emotional states.

If you’ve experienced trauma, working with a trained somatic therapist or a psychedelic guide familiar with body-based methods can be invaluable. They can help you titrate experiences, offering grounding exercises like deep belly breathing, orienting to the room, or using touch (such as holding your own hands) to anchor you during intense moments. These strategies reduce overwhelm and build a sense of safety, which is crucial during the vulnerability of a psychedelic journey.

Another practical tip is setting intention before the session focused on body awareness. Say something like, “I will notice the sensations in my body and breathe through any discomfort.” This primes your mind to include the body’s voice in the unfolding experience, rather than drifting solely through mental landscapes. The more you practice, the easier it becomes to track the nervous system’s subtle messages, making your psychedelic sessions richer and more integrated.

Common Challenges When Combining Somatic Experiencing and Psychedelic Therapy

It’s not always smooth sailing when blending somatic experiencing with psychedelic work. One common challenge is overwhelm. Psychedelics can open floodgates of sensations and emotions that feel too intense to manage. Without proper grounding tools, the body’s responses may trigger panic, dissociation, or shutdown. That’s why pacing is critical - don’t rush into heavy somatic work during or immediately after a trip. Gradual tuning in is safer and more sustainable.

Another hurdle is resistance - our bodies and minds sometimes reject paying attention to painful sensations. It can feel easier to dissociate or intellectualize than to stay with discomfort. I’ve witnessed many people push away bodily signals during trips, only to find the same tension or trauma resurfacing later. Learning to gently invite the body’s voice with compassion, rather than forcing or fighting it, helps reduce resistance.

Some people also struggle with feeling “too much” physical agitation during psychedelic experiences, confusing natural somatic discharge for pathology or panic. Trembling, shaking, or gut rumblings might feel alarming. Knowing these are often signs of the nervous system releasing stuck energy can be reassuring. Techniques like grounding through the senses - feeling your feet on the floor, holding a textured object, or focusing on external sounds - can help anchor you safely.

There’s also a learning curve around interpretation. Sometimes bodily sensations don’t clearly map onto emotions or memories, which can be frustrating. Somatic experiencing isn’t about forcing meaning but about being curious and present. It’s okay to feel sensations without naming them right away. Over time, patterns emerge naturally.

Lastly, integration can be tricky. After the session, it’s common to feel disconnected from the body again as daily life calls you back. Building regular somatic practices - like yoga, breathwork, or mindful movement - helps maintain connection and supports ongoing healing. If you find yourself stuck, seeking guidance from a trauma-informed somatic practitioner can make all the difference.

Making Somatic Experiencing Part of Everyday Life Post-Psychedelics

The psychedelic session is just the beginning. True healing unfolds as you integrate insights and somatic shifts into your daily life. One of the best ways to do this is by embedding somatic practices into your routine. Simple breathwork exercises, like the “5-5-5” method - inhale for five seconds, hold for five, exhale for five - can recalibrate your nervous system in stressful moments. Doing this daily keeps you grounded long after the medicine wears off.

Movement is another powerful tool. I encourage people to find ways to move their bodies that feel good - whether that’s dancing, walking in nature, stretching, or gentle yoga. Movement helps release residual tension and reinforces the body-mind connection. It also reminds you that healing isn’t confined to a treatment session but is ongoing and embodied.

Keeping a somatic journal, as mentioned earlier, creates a space to track sensations, moods, and breakthroughs. Writing down physical experiences alongside emotional ones helps build a bridge between body and mind. Your journal becomes a safe container where you can explore without judgment and notice subtle shifts over time.

Community and support matter too. Groups focused on somatic practices or psychedelic integration often provide a sense of belonging and shared understanding. Talking about bodily experiences in these spaces diminishes shame and normalizes the sometimes strange sensations that arise after therapy.

Lastly, I want to emphasize patience. Somatic healing is rarely linear. You might feel breakthroughs one day and blockages the next. That ebb and flow is part of the process. With consistent attention to your body, you build resilience and deepen your self-trust. The body remembers healing just as much as it remembers trauma.

A Thought to Sit With: The Body as the Keeper of Our Stories

Here’s something I keep coming back to: the body is not just a vessel for our consciousness but an archive of our lived experiences. Every tension, every tremor tells a story. Psychedelic therapy can crack open the door to those stories, but somatic experiencing hands us the key to step inside and listen.

Imagine your body as an old library. Some books are dusty and hidden, their pages stuck together by years of neglect. Psychedelics are like a gentle floodlight illuminating the shelves, revealing what was once unseen. Somatic experiencing is the patient librarian, helping you open the books slowly, turn the pages carefully, and understand their meaning without being overwhelmed. It’s an invitation to hold your pain and your healing in equal measure.

This relationship with the body challenges the dominant cultural narrative that treats healing as a purely mental task. It invites a richer, more compassionate way of relating to ourselves. To feel the ache in your chest as a messenger, not an enemy. To notice the quiver in your hands as a sign of life moving through you, not a threat. That shift in perspective changes everything.

As you continue your journey with psychedelic medicine, whether just starting or deep into your practice, consider making somatic experiencing part of your toolkit. It may not be flashy or obvious, but it’s one of the most faithful companions you’ll find. Your body remembers the way forward, even when your mind doesn’t. Sitting with that truth, in breath and stillness, might be the most radical act of healing there is.
